t_segments.topic: 个人品格与未来成功：智力、精力与正直
t_segments.summary: 巴菲特向学生们强调正直、智慧和精力在未来成功中的重要性。他通过一个“购买同学10%股份”的思维实验，阐述了品格远胜于智商和成绩，并鼓励大家培养受人尊敬的品质。

t_segments.topic: 投资哲学：雪茄烟蒂与卓越企业
t_segments.summary: 面对日本投资机会，巴菲特阐述了两种投资策略：一是“雪茄烟蒂式”投资，寻找被低估但仅剩一口价值的便宜股票；二是投资“卓越企业”，这类企业拥有强大的竞争优势，时间是其朋友，即使买贵一点长期也能获得丰厚回报。

t_segments.topic: 聪明人为何做蠢事：长期资本管理公司案例
t_segments.summary: 巴菲特回顾了长期资本管理公司（LTCM）的危机与救援，指出其创始人拥有极高智商和丰富经验，却因过度冒险而破产。他强调了“不要为不需要的钱，去冒你拥有且需要的钱的风险”这一原则，并反思了过度依赖数学模型和盲点可能带来的灾难性后果。

t_segments.topic: 财富与幸福：热爱工作与生活本质
t_segments.summary: 巴菲特分享了他对财富和幸福的看法，认为金钱对生活质量的提升有限。他鼓励学生选择自己热爱的工作，而非仅仅为了简历好看，因为只有热爱才能带来持续的激情和满足感，并警告不要为了金钱而牺牲原则或快乐。

t_segments.topic: 卓越企业的护城河：可理解性与竞争优势
t_segments.summary: 巴菲特阐述了他选择投资标的的核心标准：可理解的业务和坚固的“护城河”（竞争优势）。他以Geico的低成本、柯达失去的“心智份额”、可口可乐不断拓宽的全球网络以及箭牌口香糖的稳定市场为例，说明了护城河对企业长期成功的重要性。

t_segments.topic: 估值与定价权：品牌心智份额的力量
t_segments.summary: 巴菲特解释了如何评估企业的定价权，强调品牌在消费者“心智份额”中的价值。他以喜诗糖果（See's Candy）为例，说明了礼品属性和情感联结如何支撑高价；通过迪士尼和可口可乐，进一步阐释了强大品牌在全球范围内的定价优势和难以复制的竞争壁垒。

t_segments.topic: 投资决策：能力圈与行业洞察
t_segments.summary: 巴菲特讨论了定性与定量分析的平衡，强调坚守“能力圈”的重要性，即只投资自己真正理解的业务。他分享了通过“闲聊法”（scuttlebutt）向客户、供应商、竞争对手学习行业知识的经验，并指出一旦理解了业务，决策往往迅速而准确。

t_segments.topic: 宏观经济与长期投资：可口可乐的百年增长
t_segments.summary: 巴菲特驳斥了宏观经济预测对长期投资的价值，认为投资者应专注于“重要且可知”的微观业务。他以可口可乐为例，解释了其“无味觉记忆”的独特优势如何支撑全球高消费量，并强调一个卓越企业即使面对短期波动，长期来看也能带来巨大回报。

t_segments.topic: 投资错误与反思：遗漏之失与坚守能力圈
t_segments.summary: 巴菲特坦诚自己的投资错误，指出最大的失误是“遗漏之失”（omission），即对已理解的优秀业务未能采取行动。他承认也曾犯下“佣金之失”（commission），如购买不具吸引力业务中的有吸引力证券。他强调从错误中学习的关键是坚守能力圈，并为每一次投资提供清晰的理由。

t_segments.topic: 华尔街的喧嚣与独立思考：伯克希尔的股息哲学
t_segments.summary: 巴菲特对比了华尔街的活跃交易文化与他推崇的独立思考和不活跃投资。他解释了伯克希尔哈撒韦不分红的策略，即通过将盈利再投资以创造超过一美元的价值。他强调了“永远持有”的投资理念，并希望吸引认同此理念的股东。

t_segments.topic: 套利交易与投资组合：集中投资的优势
t_segments.summary: 巴菲特回顾了其早年参与套利交易的经历，但指出这并非其核心策略。他阐述了对冲基金经理和普通投资者在分散投资上的不同策略：非专业人士应广泛分散（如指数基金），而专业投资者则应集中投资于少数几家真正理解的卓越企业，并以可口可乐、宝洁、麦当劳和吉列为例，分析了不同公司的竞争优势。

t_segments.topic: 行业洞察与市场态度：房地产、公用事业与“卵巢彩票”
t_segments.summary: 巴菲特讨论了对电力公用事业和房地产证券化（REITs）的看法，因监管和结构复杂性而选择不投资。他重申了对市场波动的态度：作为净储蓄者，应乐见股价下跌。最后，他以“卵巢彩票”的比喻，深刻阐述了人生中运气的重要性，以及选择热爱的工作和与喜欢的人共事是实现幸福的关键。

p_segments.insight: 在识人或判断一个人是否值得长期投资时，正直的品格远比智商和精力更重要。没有正直，聪明和精力充沛反而会带来破坏性。

p_segments.insight: 品格和行为习惯并非天生，而是后天选择和培养的结果。年轻时改变坏习惯远比年老时容易，因为习惯的锁链在初期轻微到难以察觉，但最终会变得沉重到无法挣脱。

p_segments.question: 如何成为一个受人尊敬、值得投资的人？
p_segments.insight: 通过观察和模仿你所敬佩的人的品质和行为，你就能培养出这些优点。你已经100%拥有自己，所以选择成为那个你愿意投资10%的人，而不是等待别人来投资你。
p_segments.transcript: And when I decide the ones that I mean, if you like uh, Ben Graham did this and Ben Franklin did it before him, but Ben Ben Graham in his low in his low teens looked around and he looked at the people he admired and he said, you know, I want to be admired, so why don't I just behave like them? And he found there was nothing impossible about behaving like them. And similarly, he he did the same thing on the reverse side in terms of getting rid of those qualities. p_segments.insight: 对于可口可乐这样拥有强大基本面的卓越企业，短期宏观经济危机（如亚洲金融危机）不会影响其未来20年的长期增长潜力，因为其产品具有全球吸引力、高性价比和持续增长的人均消费量。
p_segments.transcript: Well, basically I love it, but but because the market for Coca-Cola products is going to grow far faster over the next 20 years uh internationally than it will in the United States. It'll grow in the United States on a per capita basis, but it's going to grow faster elsewhere. So the fact that it it's going to be a tough period for who knows, 3 months or 3 years, but it won't be tough for 20 years. I mean, people are still going to they're they're you know, they're going to work productively around the world and they're going to find that this is a a a bargain product in terms of uh of the portion of their working day that they have to give up in order to have one of these or or better yet five of them a day like I do. Uh, it's a, you know, this is a product in 1936 when I first bought six of those for a quarter and sold them for a nickel each and it was in a 6 and 1/2 oz bottle and you paid a 2% 2 cent deposit on the bottle. That was a 6 and 1/2 oz bottle for a nickel at that time. It's now a 12 oz can which if you buy it on on weekends or if you buy it in bigger quantities so so much money doesn't go to the packaging. I mean you essentially can buy the 12 oz for not much more than 20 cents. So, you're paying not much more than twice the per oz price of 1936 and it is a product that's gotten cheaper and cheaper and cheaper in relative to people's earning power over the years and which people love in in in 200 countries you have the per capita use going up every year for a product that's over 100 years old and that dominates the market. I mean that is is unbelievable.
p_segments.quote: It's a product that's gotten cheaper and cheaper relative to people's earning power over the years and which people love in 200 countries.
p_segments.quoteCn: 这是一款相对于人们的购买力越来越便宜、同时在 200 个国家被人们热爱的产品。

p_segments.question: 为什么有些产品能让人反复消费而不生厌？
p_segments.insight: 可乐的巨大商业成功在于其独特的“无味觉记忆”属性，这让消费者可以高频次、大剂量饮用而不感到厌倦，从而支撑起惊人的人均消费量和全球市场规模，这是其他有味觉记忆的产品难以复制的。
p_segments.transcript: One thing that people don't understand is one thing that makes this product that is worth tens and tens of billions of dollars is one simple fact about about really all colas but we'll call it Coca-Cola for the moment. It happens to be a name I like. Uh, cola has no taste memory. You can drink one of these at 9:00, 11:00, 3:00 in the afternoon, 5:00. The one at 5:00 will taste just as good to you as the one you drank earlier in the morning. You can't do that with cream soda, root beer, orange, grape, you name it. All of those things accumulate on you. Most foods and and and and beverages are accumulate on you. You get sick of them after a while. And if you if you eat I mean we get these people that go to work for us at See's Candy and we tell them they can eat all the candy they want. The first day they go crazy but after a week they're eating about the same amount they'd eat if if they were buying it because chocolate accumulates on it. Everything accumulates on There is no taste memory to cola. And that means that you get people around the world that are heavy users that'll drink five a day or diet Coke maybe, you know, seven or eight a day or something of the sort. They'll never do that with with other products. So, you get this incredible per capita consumption. The average person in in this part of the world uh, well, maybe a little north of here, drinks about 64 oz of liquid a day. And you can have all 64 oz of that be Coke and you will not get fed up with Coke if you like it to start with in the least but if you do that with almost anything else, if you eat just one product all day, you'll you'll get sick of it after a while and and and uh, it's it's a huge factor so that today over 1 billion 8 oz servings of Coca-Cola products will be sold in the world and that will grow year by year. It'll grow in every country virtually. It'll grow on a per capita basis and 20 years from now it'll grow a lot faster internationally than in the US.
p_segments.quote: Cola has no taste memory. You can drink one at 9:00, 11:00, 3:00, 5:00. The one at 5:00 will taste just as good as the one you drank in the morning.
p_segments.quoteCn: 可乐没有味觉记忆。你可以在早上 9 点、11 点、下午 3 点、5 点各喝一杯，5 点那杯味道仍然和早上一样好。

p_segments.question: 投资时应该关注时机还是企业本身？
p_segments.insight: 对于一个卓越企业，如果你对它的本质（“what”）判断正确，那么市场时机（“when”）和短期宏观事件几乎不重要。可口可乐的历史证明，即使经历股价腰斩、大萧条和战争，一个好企业长期复利的回报会压倒一切短期噪音。
p_segments.transcript: I mean it uh, uh, Coca-Cola went public in I think it's 1919. Stock sold for $40 a share. Went back before that it was the Candler family and they they went back. They bought it for 2,000 bucks the whole business Asa Candler back in the late 1880s in a couple of purchases. So, now it goes public in 1919, $40 a share. One year later it's selling for $19, going down 50% in one year. Now you might think that's some kind of disaster and you might think that sugar prices increased and the bottlers were rebellious and a whole bunch of things. You could always find a few reasons why that wasn't the ideal moment to buy it. Years later you'd have seen the Great Depression and you'd have seen World War II and you'd seen sugar rationing and you'd seen thermonuclear weapons and the whole thing. There's always a reason but in the end if you'd bought one share for 40 bucks and reinvested the dividends it would be worth about 5 million now. And that factor so overrides anything else. I mean if you're right about the business you'll make a lot of money and and the timing part of it is very is is a very tricky thing. So, I don't worry about any given event if I've got a wonderful business. Uh, you know, whether what it does to the next year or something of the sort. The um, you know, uh, price controls have been in this country various times and that's that's followed up even the best of businesses. I mean I wouldn't be able to raise the price on December 26th of See's Candy if we had price controls and we've had them in this country. But that doesn't make it a lousy business if that happens to happen because you're not going to have price controls forever. Uh, we had them in the early in the early '70s. So, it it the wonderful business you know, you can figure out what will happen. You can't figure out when it will happen. You don't want to focus too much on when. You want to focus on what. If you're right about what, you don't have to worry about when very much.
p_segments.quote: If you bought one share for 40 bucks and reinvested the dividends, it would be worth about 5 million now. The timing part of it is a very tricky thing.
p_segments.quoteCn: 如果你用 40 美元买一股可口可乐并再投资股息，现在它值大约 500 万美元。时机把握是非常 tricky 的事。

p_segments.question: 投资中最大的错误是什么？是错失良机还是错买股票？
p_segments.insight: 投资中最大的错误不是买错，而是错失了那些你本可以理解并采取行动的优秀机会（遗漏之失）。同时，也要警惕因为条款诱人而买入你不理解的业务（佣金之失）。
p_segments.transcript: Well, the interesting thing about the mistakes is that in investments at least for me and for my partner Charlie Munger the biggest mistakes have not been mistakes of of commission, they've been mistakes of omission. They're where we knew enough about the business to do something and for one reason or another we sat there sucking our thumbs instead of doing something. And so, we've we've passed up things where we could have made billions and billions of dollars from things we understood. Forget about things we don't understand. We don't In fact, I could make billions out of Microsoft doesn't mean anything because I never understand Microsoft. But if I can make billions out of healthcare stocks then I should make it and I didn't, you know, when when when the Clinton healthcare program was proposed and they all went in the tank. Um, we should have made a ton of money out of that. Uh, because I could understand it. I didn't make it. I should have made a ton of money out of Fannie Mae back in the mid-'80s because I understood it and I didn't do it. Those are billion dollar mistakes or multi-billion dollar mistakes that that generally accepted accounting principles don't pick up. Uh, the mistakes you see the mistakes you see we we made a it isn't we, I made a mistake uh, buying US Air preferred some years ago. I mean it I had a lot of money around. I make mistakes when I get cash. Charlie tells me to go to a bar instead. You know, don't hang around the office. But I I hang around the office and I got money in my pocket I do something dumb. And it happens every time and and uh, so I I bought this thing. Nobody made me buy it. I now have a 800 number I call every time I think about buying stock in an airline and they talk me down. They say, you know, I say I'm worn, I'm an aeroholic and then the guy says, you know, keep talking, don't hang up, you know, and don't do anything rash. Finally I get over it. Uh, but I but I I I bought it, you know, and uh, it looked like we were going to lose all our money in that and we came very close to losing all our money and and you can say we deserved to lose all our money. And we bought it because it was an attractive security but it was in a not in an attractive business. I did the same thing with Solomon. I bought an attractive security in a in a business that I wouldn't have bought the equity in. So, you can say that that's one form of mistake buying something because you like the terms when you don't like the business that well. And I've I've I've done that in the past and probably do it again.
p_segments.quote: The biggest mistakes have not been mistakes of commission, they've been mistakes of omission. We passed up things where we could have made billions and billions of dollars.
p_segments.quoteCn: 最大的错误不是做错了什么，而是该做的事没做。我们错过了本可以赚几十亿美元的机会。

p_segments.question: 如何避免投资失误，坚守能力圈？
p_segments.insight: 避免投资失误的关键是坚守自己的能力圈，只投资你真正理解的业务。每一次决策都必须能清晰地阐述理由，如果不能，就不要行动。
p_segments.transcript: the big thing to do is stick with the businesses you understand and uh, so if there's a generic mistake of of getting outside of your circle of competence and you know, buying something because somebody tips you on it or something of the sort in in an area you don't know anything about. I mean that you should learn something from that which is that you stay with what you can figure out yourself. I mean you really want your decision making to be by looking in the mirror and saying to yourself, I'm buying 100 shares of General Motors at 55 because and I mean it's your responsibility if you're buying it and there's got to be a reason. And if you can't state the reason, you shouldn't buy it. If it's because somebody told you about it at a cocktail party, not good enough, you know, I mean there's just it's got to be something, you know, can't be because the the volume you know, the chart looks good on it or anything like that. It's got got to be a reason you'd buy the business. And we that we stick to pretty pretty carefully. That's one of the things Ben Graham taught me.
p_segments.quote: You've got to have a reason. If you can't state the reason, you shouldn't buy it.
p_segments.quoteCn: 你必须有一个理由。如果你说不出理由，就不该买。

p_segments.question: 如何避免宏观经济预测对投资决策的干扰？
p_segments.insight: 投资应专注于可理解且重要的微观企业因素，而非不可预测的宏观经济趋势，因为宏观预测对具体投资决策没有实际意义，反而可能导致错失良机。
p_segments.transcript: The question is about what's going to happen to interest rates and where we're going in the world. I don't think about the macro stuff. You know, I I just important What you really want to do in investments is figure out what's important and knowable. If it's unimportant or unknowable, you you forget about it. What you talk about is important but in my view it's not knowable. Understanding Coca-Cola is knowable or Wrigley or Eastman Kodak or anything. I mean you can understand those businesses. That's knowable. And whether it turns out to be important depends on where your valuation leads you in the current price and all of that. But, we have never either bought a business or not bought a business because of any macro feeling of any kind. We don't read things about predictions about interest rates or business or anything like that because it doesn't make any difference. I mean, let's say in 1972 when we bought See's Candy, I think maybe Nixon put on the price controls a little bit later. Let's say we'd seen it. But, so what? We would have missed the chance to buy something for 25 million that's earning 60 million pre-tax now. I mean, we we don't want to pass up the chance to do something intelligent because of some prediction about something that we're no good at it anyway. So, we just don't we don't read or listen to or do anything in relation to to macro factors at all. Zero. And the typical investment counseling organization goes out and they give you the they bring out their economists, they trot them out, and he gives you this big macro picture, and then they start working from there on down. In our view, that's nonsense. It uh uh and if if, you know, if Alan Greenspan was on one side of me and Bob Rubin on the other side, they're both whispering in my ear exactly what they're going to do for the next 12 months, wouldn't make any difference to me in what I pay for Executive Jet or General Reinsurance or anything else I do.
p_segments.quote: Figure out what's important and knowable. If it's unimportant or unknowable, you forget about it.
p_segments.quoteCn: 找出什么是重要的、可知的。如果它不重要或不可知，就忘掉它。

p_segments.question: 为什么华尔街的活跃交易环境不利于投资成功？
p_segments.insight: 华尔街以交易活动盈利，而投资者通过不活跃的长期持有盈利。过度活跃的市场环境会刺激投资者频繁操作，导致亏损，因此应远离此类环境，保持独立思考。
p_segments.transcript: But, the best way to get to think about investments is to be in a room with no one else and just think. And if that doesn't work, nothing else is going to work. Uh and the disadvantage of being in any kind of a market type environment in Wall Street would be the extreme is that you get overstimulated. You think you have to do something every day. I mean, the Campbell family paid 2,000 bucks for this company, and that you don't have to do much else if you you have to pick one of those. And the trick then is not to do anything else. Even not to sell it in 1919, which they the family did later on it. Uh what you're looking for is some way to get one good idea a year, you know, and then and then ride it to its full potential. And that's very hard to do in an environment where people are shouting prices back and forth every 5 minutes and shoving reports under your nose and all that. Because Wall Street makes its money on activity. You make your money on inactivity, you know. I mean, if everybody in this room trades their portfolio around every day with every other person, you know, you're all going to end up broke. And then and and the intermediary is going to end up with all the money. On the other hand, if you all own stock in a in group of average businesses and just sit here for the next 50 years, you'll end up with a fair amount of money, and your broker will be broke. So, his act his activity is is He's like a doctor who gets paid on how often he gets you to change pills, I mean, basically. I mean, if he gives you one pill and it cures you the rest of your life, you know, he's got one sale, one transaction, and and that's it. But, if he can convince you that changing pills every day is the way to great health, uh it'll be great for him and the prescriptionists, and and you'll be out a lot of money, and you won't be any healthier. You'll be a lot worse off financially. So, it you want to stay away from any environment that stimulates activity.
p_segments.quote: Wall Street makes its money on activity. You make your money on inactivity.
p_segments.quoteCn: 华尔街靠交易活动赚钱。你靠不交易赚钱。

p_segments.question: 公司不分红，如何为股东创造价值？
p_segments.insight: 伯克希尔哈撒韦不分红是因为公司有能力将留存的每一美元转化为超过一美元的价值，这比直接分红对股东更有利，是公司运营的核心目标。
p_segments.transcript: But, the the real question is whether we can keep retaining dollar bills and turning them into more than a dollar at at a decent rate. And and that's what we try to do, and and and Charlie Munger and I have our our money in it to do that. That's all we'll get paid for doing. We won't take any options, we won't take any salaries to pay government or anything. We'll ride around in the plane. Uh but, the that's what we're trying to do. It gets harder all the time. The the more money we manage, the harder it is to do that. And we would do way better percentage-wise with Berkshire if it were 1/100 the present size. But, it is a it is run for its owners, but it isn't run to give them dividends because so far every dollar that we've earned and could have paid out, we've turned into more than a dollar. It's worth more than a dollar to keep it. And therefore, it'd be silly to pay it out. Even if everybody was tax-free that owned it, it would have been a mistake to pay dividends at Berkshire because so far the dollar bills retained have turned into more than a dollar. But, there's no guarantee that that happens in the future, and at some point the game runs out on that. Uh uh But, it it is the goal of I mean, that is what the business is about.
p_segments.quote: Every dollar that we've earned and could have paid out, we've turned into more than a dollar. It'd be silly to pay it out.
p_segments.quoteCn: 我们赚的每一美元，如果保留下来能变成超过一美元，那支付股息就是愚蠢的。

p_segments.question: 如何选择值得长期持有的公司？
p_segments.insight: 理想的投资是购买那些你愿意永远持有的企业，并吸引同样抱有长期持有信念的股东，因为真正的价值增长来自于企业持续创造现金流，而非短期交易或设定价格目标。
p_segments.transcript: know, or 15 years. It I mean, there could be something happen, but I I I would think that chances of that are almost nil. So, what we really want to do is buy businesses that we would be happy to own forever. It's the same way I feel about people buy Berkshire. I want people to buy Berkshire to plan to hold it forever. They may not for one reason or another, but I want them at the time they buy it to think they are buying a business that they're going to own forever. And I don't say that's the only way to buy things. It's just that that's the group I want to have join me because I don't want to have a changing group all the time. I measure Berkshire by how little activity there is in it. If I if I had a church and I was the preacher and half the congregation left every Sunday, I I wouldn't say, "Oh, this is marvelous cuz I have all this liquidity among my members, you know. This terrific turnover, you know." Uh I would I would I would rather get a church where all the seats are filled, you know, every Sunday by the same people. Well, that's the way we look at the businesses we buy. We want to buy something that we're really happy to own um virtually forever. And we can't find a lot of those. And back when I started, I had way more ideas than money. So, I was just constantly having to sell what I thought was the least attractive stock in order to buy something that I just discovered that looked even cheaper. But, that's not our problem really now. And so, we hope we're buying businesses that we're just as happy with 5 years from now as as now. And if we ever found some huge acquisition, you know, then we'd have to sell something. Uh maybe to make that acquisition, but that that would be a very pleasant pleasant problem to have. We never buy something with a price target in mind. I mean, we never buy something at 30 saying if it goes to 40, we'll sell it or 50 or 60 or 100. We just don't do it that way anymore than when we buy a private business like See's Candy for 25 million, we don't say to ourselves, "If it ever If we ever get an offer of 50 million for this business, we'd sell it." That That's just not the way to look at a business. We look at a business as "Is this going to keep producing more and more and more money over time?" And if the answer to that is yes, you don't need to ask any more questions.
p_segments.quote: We want to buy businesses that we would be happy to own forever. We never buy something with a price target in mind.
p_segments.quoteCn: 我们想买入那些愿意永远拥有的企业。我们从不带着目标价去买东西。

p_segments.question: 普通投资者和专业投资者应该如何选择分散投资策略？
p_segments.insight: 对于非专业投资者，应采取极致分散策略，如投资低成本指数基金；而对于有能力深入评估企业的专业投资者，过度分散是巨大错误，应集中投资于少数几家真正理解的卓越企业。
p_segments.transcript: The question is about diversification, and I've got a dual answer to that. If you are not a professional investor, if your if your goal is not to manage money in such a way as to get a significantly better return than the world. Uh then I believe in extreme diversification. I mean, if if So, I believe 98 or 99% maybe more than 99% of people who invest uh should extensively diversify and not trade. So, that leads them to an index fund type of a decision with very low cost. Cuz all they're going to do is own a part of America, and they made a decision that owning part of America is worthwhile. I don't quarrel with that at all. That is the way they should approach it unless they want to bring an intensity to the game to make it a decision and start evaluating businesses. But once you're in the business of evaluating businesses, and and you decide that you're going to bring the effort and intensity and uh uh and time involved to get that job done, then I think that diversification is a terrible mistake and at any degree. And uh I got asked that question when I was at SunTrust the other day, and uh if you really know businesses, you probably shouldn't own more than six of them. I mean, if you can identify six wonderful businesses, that is all the diversification you need, and you're going to make a lot of money, and I will guarantee you that going into a seventh one is going to, rather than putting more money in your first one, it's got to be a terrible mistake. Very few people have gotten rich on their seventh best idea. Now, but a lot of people got rich on their best idea. Yeah. So, I I would uh I would say that for anybody working with normal capital who really knows the businesses they've gone into, a six is plenty. And and they probably have half of it in what I like best. I don't diversify personally. I mean, and and uh uh all the people I know that have done well, uh with the exception of what we mentioned Walter Schloss earlier. Walter diversifies a lot. He owns a little of everything. I call him Noah, you know, he's got two of everything.
p_segments.quote: If you are not a professional investor, I believe in extreme diversification. If you really know businesses, diversification is a terrible mistake. Six is plenty.
p_segments.quoteCn: 如果你不是专业投资者，我主张极度分散。但如果你真的懂企业，分散是个可怕的错误。六只就够了。

p_segments.question: 如何判断一家公司是否拥有长期增长和定价权？
p_segments.insight: 可口可乐拥有比宝洁更强的单位增长潜力和定价权，因为它能通过微小的价格上涨带来巨额利润，且产品消费习惯更稳定，这使其成为更具长期优势的投资标的。

Actually, Procter & Gamble is a little more it'd be more diversified among product lines, but I would feel sure of Coke than Procter & Gamble. I wouldn't be unhappy if somebody told me I had to own Procter & Gamble during that 20-year period. I mean, that would be in my top 5% because they they are not going to get killed. Uh you know, but and I would feel better about the unit growth and the pricing power of a Coke over 20 or 30 years than I would about a Procter & Gamble. Right now, the pricing power might be tough, but you think of billion billion servings a day, you know. An extra penny, $10 million a day. You know. We own 8% of it. That's that's $800,000 a day for Berkshire Hathaway. You get another penny out of this stuff. Doesn't seem impossible, does it? I mean, it it's worth another penny, but uh it doesn't Right now, it'd be a mistake to try and get it in most markets, but over time, Coke will make more per serving than it does now. 20 years from now, I'll guarantee they'll make more per serving, and they'll be selling a whole lot more servings. I don't know how many. I don't know how much more, but I know that. Uh P&G's main products, I don't think they have the kind of dominance, and they don't have the kind of unit growth. But they're but they're good businesses. You know, I I would not be unhappy uh if you told me that I had to put my family's net worth in P&G, and that was the only stock I could own, I wouldn't you know, that I might prefer some other names, but there aren't 100 other names I would prefer.
p_segments.quote: An extra penny, $10 million a day. We own 8% of it. That's $800,000 a day for Berkshire Hathaway.

p_segments.question: 如何评估一个产品是否具备不依赖促销的独立销售能力？
p_segments.insight: 真正强大的产品能够独立销售，不依赖促销或价格吸引力，例如吉列剃须刀；而像麦当劳这样的快餐业务，因消费习惯和竞争激烈，更易陷入促销依赖，从根本上削弱了其产品本身的强大性。

p_segments.question: 面对一个产品吸引人但盈利模式不确定的行业，投资决策该如何做？
p_segments.insight: 即使一个行业的产品具有确定性和吸引力，如果对解除管制后的未来盈利模式和谁能赚钱不清晰，就应避免投资，因为不确定性会侵蚀收益。

And I don't for sure see how who benefits and how much. I mean, obviously the guy with very low cost power, some guy's got hydro power, you know, at 2 cents a kilowatt or something like that. p_segments.question: 选择投资标的时，公司规模是关键考量因素吗？
p_segments.insight: 投资决策的核心不应是公司规模大小，而是能否理解业务、是否喜欢管理层以及价格是否吸引人；只有资金量极大时才会被迫限制于大公司。

p_segments.question: 投资房地产信托基金（REITs）有哪些潜在陷阱？
p_segments.insight: 房地产信托基金（REITs）虽避免了双重征税，但通常运营成本高昂，侵蚀了收益；且其流动性不如股票，易被错误估值；对于有足够资本的投资者，直接持有物业通常更优。

p_segments.question: 为什么长期投资者应该乐见市场下跌？
p_segments.insight: 作为长期净储蓄者和股票买家，投资者应将股市视为购买商品的超市，乐见股价下跌以获得更优惠的买入价格，而非因短期波动而情绪化。
p_segments.transcript: Yeah, I've got no idea where the market's going to go. I I prefer it going down, but I but I have you know, my preferences have nothing to do with it. They the market knows nothing about my feelings. Uh you know, that's one of the first things you have to learn with a stock. You know, you buy a hundred shares of General Motors. Now, all of a sudden you have this feeling about General Motors. I mean, if it goes down, you may be mad at it. You may say, well, if it just goes up to what I paid for it, you know, my life will be wonderful again or if it goes up, you may say how smart you were and you and General Motors have this love affair and everything. You've got all these feelings. Stock doesn't know you own it. Stock just sits there. It doesn't care what you paid. It doesn't care that you owned it or anything. So, any feeling I have about the market is not reciprocated. I mean, it is the ultimate Now, it is it is very cold shoulder we're talking about here and anybody that is going to be a net saver practically everybody in this room is more likely to be a net buyer of stocks over the next 10 years than they are a net seller. So, every one of you should prefer lower prices. I mean, if you're going to be a net eater of hamburger in the next 10 years, you want hamburger to go down unless you're a cattle producer. p_segments.question: 如何理解人生中的运气，并做出能带来幸福的选择？
p_segments.insight: 人生成功和幸福的起点很大程度上取决于“卵巢彩票”带来的先天运气；在此基础上，选择热爱的工作并只与喜欢的人共事，是实现幸福的关键，这甚至比赚取巨额财富更重要。
